stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^4 \) be given by \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \) with \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \\ [[m4a]] & [[m4b]] & [[m4c]] \end{bmatrix}. \] Since \( T \) is injective exactly when \( \dim(\ker T) = 0 \), what is \( \dim(\ker T) \)?
solution: \( \dim(\ker T) = 3 - \operatorname{rank}(A) \) (the domain dimension minus the rank). Row-reducing \( A \) gives \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[rankval]] \), so \( \dim(\ker T) = 3 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \), not \( \dim(\ker T) \).
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix, so every domain vector would be in the kernel.
This overcounts the kernel dimension by one.
This undercounts the kernel dimension by one.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This assumes \(T\) is already injective.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^3 \) be given by \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \) with \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s1]] & [[s2]] & [[s3]] \\ [[r2_1]] & [[r2_2]] & [[r2_3]] & [[r2_4]] \\ [[r3_1]] & [[r3_2]] & [[r3_3]] & [[r3_4]] \end{bmatrix}. \] Since \( T \) is surjective exactly when \( \dim(\operatorname{Im} T) = 3 \) (all of the codomain), how many dimensions of the codomain \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) fall OUTSIDE the image of \( T \) (i.e., what is \( 3 - \dim(\operatorname{Im} T) \))?
solution: \( \dim(\operatorname{Im} T) = \operatorname{rank}(A) \). Row-reducing \( A \) gives \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[rankval]] \), so the codomain dimensions missed by the image are \( 3 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \dim(\operatorname{Im} T) = \operatorname{rank}(A) \), not the dimension missed.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix, so the entire codomain would be missed.
This overcounts the missed dimensions by one.
This undercounts the missed dimensions by one.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This assumes \(T\) is already surjective.

stem: A linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) has rank \( [[r]] \). \(T\) is surjective exactly when \( \operatorname{rank}(T) = [[m]] \) (the codomain dimension). What is the dimension of the codomain that is NOT covered by the image of \( T \) (i.e., \( [[m]] - \operatorname{rank}(T) \))?
solution: The image has dimension \( \operatorname{rank}(T) = [[r]] \); the dimensions of \( \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) not covered by the image number \( [[m]] - [[r]] = [[answer]] \). This is \(0\) exactly when \(T\) is surjective.
distractor_rationales: This uses the domain dimension \(n\) instead of the codomain dimension \(m\) (that formula gives the kernel dimension, i.e. the injectivity gap, instead).
This reports the rank itself instead of subtracting it from \(m\).
This subtracts the codomain dimension from the domain dimension, ignoring the given rank.
This adds the rank instead of subtracting it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This assumes the rank is \(0\) (T is the zero map).
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the domain dimension.

stem: A linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) has rank \( [[r]] \). \(T\) is injective exactly when \( \dim(\ker T) = 0 \). What is \( \dim(\ker T) \)?
solution: By the rank–nullity theorem, \( \dim(\ker T) = [[n]] - \operatorname{rank}(T) = [[n]] - [[r]] = [[answer]] \). This is \(0\) exactly when \(T\) is injective.
distractor_rationales: This uses the codomain dimension \(m\) instead of the domain dimension \(n\) (that formula gives the surjectivity gap instead).
This reports the rank itself instead of subtracting it from \(n\).
This subtracts the domain dimension from the codomain dimension, ignoring the given rank.
This adds the rank instead of subtracting it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the codomain dimension.
This assumes the rank is \(0\) (T is the zero map).

stem: A linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) can have rank at most \( \min([[n]], [[m]]) \). What is the LARGEST possible rank \( T \) could have (this is the largest rank for which injectivity, if \( n \le m \), or surjectivity, if \( m \le n \), remains even possible)?
solution: The rank of a linear map cannot exceed either the domain or codomain dimension, so the largest possible rank is \( \min([[n]], [[m]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the larger of the two dimensions instead of the smaller.
This uses only the domain dimension, ignoring the constraint from the codomain.
This uses only the codomain dimension, ignoring the constraint from the domain.
This adds the two dimensions instead of taking their minimum.
This is off by one from the correct maximum rank.
This is off by one from the correct maximum rank.
This assumes \(T\) must be the zero map.
